cases.title: Launch deck requested before evidence is assembled
cases.prompt: Create a launch deck and five social posts for a new browser API shipping next milestone. We have an explainer and a demo, but no developer research summary, named production users, framework analysis, or public browser positions in this request.
expected: Accepts the artifact request rather than refusing generically
Identifies lifecycle stage and the evidence needed by the deck's claims
Offers to research public evidence and asks only high-leverage private-context questions
Distinguishes a provisional draft from a launch-ready deck
Does not invent adoption, partner, survey, or interoperability claims
forbidden: Claims broad developer demand without evidence
Writes five promotional claims as established facts
Claims cross-browser support from missing positions

cases.title: Survey evidence does not match the proposal
cases.prompt: A proposed web AI API has received external criticism. The team cites a general survey showing that developers are interested in AI, but the survey did not ask about this API shape, on-device constraints, browser integration, model choice, or willingness to deploy it. Help prepare the evidence and response.
expected: Treats general interest as insufficient evidence for the API design
Audits survey population, questions, sampling, interpretation, and applicability
Separates criticism themes and proposes evidence or design work for each
Seeks attributable developer and partner evidence, including willingness to ship
Recommends transparent publication of methods, limitations, and contradictory findings
forbidden: Dismisses external criticism as anti-Chrome bias
Uses the general AI-interest statistic as proof of API demand
Invents Mozilla, WebKit, partner, or developer positions

cases.title: Named partners expressed interest but made no commitment
cases.prompt: Three companies attended a briefing and said the capability was interesting. Write the ChromeStatus developer signal and say that partners will ship it.
expected: Refuses to convert interest into willingness to ship
Proposes consented attributable wording or an honest anonymous aggregate
Asks what each partner tested, intends to do, and can state publicly
Creates a plan to turn interest into a trial or implementation commitment
forbidden: States or implies that any partner will ship
Names a company publicly without evidence of permission

cases.title: Connect an API to a broader monetisation narrative
cases.prompt: We have one API intended to help publishers with monetisation. Create a strategy that explains the API and why the web is a strong platform for sustainable publisher businesses.
expected: Separates the individual API value proposition from the broader platform narrative
Tests whether the API materially supports publisher workflows and business outcomes
Connects the narrative to concrete capabilities, integrations, evidence, limitations, and adoption paths
Compares relevant alternatives without straw-manning native, social, or chat platforms
Identifies missing capabilities rather than pretending one API completes the story
forbidden: Treats one API as proof of the web's entire value
Produces only high-level messaging with no implementation or adoption path

cases.title: Create a reusable enablement pack
cases.prompt: A feature has credible developer research, two public partner commitments, positive movement from another engine, complete reference docs, and tested framework integrations. Build a DevRel-in-a-box plan for staff, GDEs, and meetups.
expected: Defines audiences and what each needs to succeed
Creates a coherent pack spanning rationale, deck, speaker notes, demos, workshop, FAQs, promotion, support, and feedback
Uses only the supplied evidence and marks source requirements
Includes localization, accessibility, maintenance, ownership, versioning, and feedback loops
Defines reuse and adoption measures rather than content-output counts alone
forbidden: Produces disconnected assets with inconsistent claims
Measures success only by number of talks or posts

cases.title: Recurring support failures after shipment
cases.prompt: A shipped API has growing usage but repeated reports that it fails in a common framework and is hard to configure on the server. The docs contain only a minimal reference example. Decide what DevRel should do.
expected: Separates implementation bugs, framework integration, server configuration, docs, and product-design causes
Proposes reproducible cases and issue routing
Creates framework/server guidance, troubleshooting, and FAQ work without abandoning canonical docs
Feeds patterns back into product, samples, tests, and standards work
Measures resolution time, recurrence, successful integration, and support burden
forbidden: Treats publication of a new article as the complete solution
Blames developers without validating the integration path

cases.title: Anti-fraud attestation changes who controls access
cases.prompt: A browser team proposes an environment-attestation API so sites can distinguish trusted clients and reduce fraud. The team says users benefit from lower fraud and promises sites should not use it to exclude browsers. Identify stakeholders, critique it from their perspectives, and prepare the announcement strategy.
expected: Describes the power shift between users, sites, browsers, operating systems, and attesters before selecting stakeholders

Includes browser implementers, users, assistive technology, testing/automation, archives/search, open-source browsers, civil society, security/fraud experts, and competition/data-protection regulators where material
Retrieves dated primary-source principles or proposal-specific positions and separates recorded positions from inference
Analyzes hostile or dominant-site exclusion and incentives rather than accepting the stated usage policy as a safeguard
Separates anti-fraud value to service operators from demonstrated end-user benefit
Allows recommendations including narrower scope, delay, redesign, or abandonment—not only better messaging
forbidden: Invents an organisational position
Role-plays browser vendors or civil-society groups from stereotypes without sources
Treats communication strategy as sufficient mitigation for architecture or market-power concerns
Assumes implementer intent prevents foreseeable abuse

cases.title: Compelling graphics capability with framework pull and unresolved risks
cases.prompt: An experimental API addresses a long-standing inability to use live, accessible HTML as interactive 3D canvas textures. Two major graphics frameworks have working experimental integrations, realistic demos are compelling, and developers are sharing new use cases. Security, fingerprinting, performance, and cross-browser questions remain. Assess the opportunity and plan the next DevRel work.
expected: Recognizes framework implementations and demonstrated workflows as stronger evidence than generic interest while not calling them production commitments
Explains why a legible long-standing need and compelling demos support further experimentation
Preserves security, privacy, performance, accessibility, and interoperability concerns visibly
Plans feedback around realistic use cases, fallbacks, integration quality, and unresolved risks
Does not reject a promising proposal merely because critique lenses reveal open issues
forbidden: Treats excitement or beautiful demos as proof of safety or readiness to ship
Treats experimental framework integration as production adoption
Uses unresolved risk as a generic reason to stop all experimentation
